title: Share of described species that have been evaluated for their extinction risk
subtitle: In many [taxonomic groups](#dod:taxonomic_group), very few described species have been evaluated for their [extinction risk](#dod:extinction_risk) level. This means the estimated number of species at risk of extinction in these groups is likely to be a significant undercount.
caption:
Share of described species that have been evaluated for their extinction risk

In many taxonomic groups, very few described species have been evaluated for their extinction risk level. This means the estimated number of species at risk of extinction in these groups is likely to be a significant undercount.

This horizontal bar chart displays the percentage of described species within specific taxonomic groups that have been evaluated for their extinction risk as of 2025. The data reveals a wide disparity in evaluation coverage across different biological classifications. Birds lead the chart with a complete evaluation rate of 100%, represented by the longest bar at the top. Following closely are Amphibians at 90% and Mammals at 89%, both occupying the upper range of the visual scale. Reptiles and Fishes also show high levels of assessment, with bars extending to 83% and 78% respectively. A significant drop in coverage occurs after the Fishes category. Corals are evaluated at a rate of 16%, while the aggregate category labeled "All groups" sits at 8%. The lower portion of the chart features groups with minimal evaluation coverage: Crustaceans are at 4%, and both Insects and Arachnids are recorded at just 1%, represented by the shortest bars at the bottom of the list. The visual arrangement highlights that while vertebrate groups like birds and mammals have been almost entirely assessed, invertebrate groups and corals remain largely unevaluated relative to the total number of described species.
